topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery person, a young man with an overly bright smile, placed the bouquet on the entryway table. They were vibrant sunflowers, their heads heavy with pollen, and a deep, almost alarming shade of crimson roses. There was no note, just the flowers resting against the polished wood. My hand, reaching for the vase, paused halfway. A prickle started at the base of my neck, a tight, cold sensation that spread quickly through my chest. I stared at the arrangement, cataloging the exact shade of yellow against the dark red. Who would do this? The silence of the apartment suddenly felt too loud, amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I backed away slowly, my gaze darting toward the door, half-expecting someone to appear and demand an explanation.

[story 2]

I found the package tucked beneath the welcome mat. It was wrapped in plain brown paper, tied with rough twine, and inside, the blooms were exquisite—a cascade of pale lavender lilies and delicate white orchids. No inscription. My fingers traced the velvety texture of a petal, a tremor running through my arm. Every instinct screamed at me to inspect the wrapping for any hidden message, any slip of paper that might offer context. I paced the small living room, my breath catching in shallow gasps. The sunlight streaming through the window seemed harsh, illuminating the fine lines of anxiety etched around my eyes. I kept touching the flowers, a desperate need for some anchor in the sudden, unsettling emptiness of the morning.

[story 3]

It was left on my desk, right beside the stack of unopened mail. A stunning arrangement of deep purple irises, their petals almost black at the edges. The absence of a card was what truly unsettled me; it felt like an intentional omission, a deliberate void. I picked one up, the stem cool against my palm, and turned it over, searching for a hidden compartment or a secret tag. My jaw tightened. I couldn't focus on anything else. The ticking of the grandfather clock in the hall seemed to accelerate, each swing of the pendulum a heavy, insistent beat against the rising tide of unease in my gut. I stood frozen, the vibrant, silent beauty of the flowers mocking the sheer lack of information.
